title: Justice

jokes.text: Jesus Beverly Christ, I'm starving.
jokes.setup: Opening scene, Selina is being briefed on presidential business
jokes.punchline: She interrupts with a dramatic, profanity-laced hunger complaint — 'Jesus Beverly Christ' as a coined oath
jokes.explanation: The invented oath 'Jesus Beverly Christ' is a Selina-specific verbal tic — profane but with a ladylike middle name that perfectly encapsulates her character. Strong character integration and economy.

jokes.text: If it was any more Kennedy-esque, it would drive you into the ocean.
jokes.setup: Staff pitches the Eastern Shore of Maryland library site as Kennedy-esque
jokes.punchline: The Kennedy-esque quality is extended to Chappaquiddick — driving you into the ocean
jokes.explanation: A darkly elegant joke — the Kennedy comparison is immediately subverted with Chappaquiddick. Excellent economy, the punchline lands in the second half of the same sentence. Sharp political satire sensibility.

jokes.text: Fittingly, I lost my back-virginity in Assateague.
jokes.setup: Eastern Shore is praised as classy
jokes.punchline: Selina contributes her own personal geographic connection — losing her 'back-virginity' there
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character comedy — only Selina Meyer would respond to a classy location pitch by casually sharing anal sex geography. The term 'back-virginity' is a perfect Selina neologism. High quotability and character specificity. This is genuinely exceptional writing.

jokes.text: I once lost my wallet in Denver.
jokes.setup: Selina's 'back-virginity in Assateague' is a TMI overshare that demands a response
jokes.punchline: Mike attempts to match the personal geography anecdote but contributes a completely banal, non-equivalent story
jokes.explanation: Six words. Perfect. Mike's desperate attempt to contribute something relatable lands as pure deadpan absurdism — he understood the assignment (share a personal geographic anecdote) but missed everything else about it. Maximum funny per word. Becomes a quotable non sequitur that defines Mike's character. One of the best jokes in the episode.

jokes.text: He was a friend to all people, especially corporations, who he legally considered to be people.
jokes.setup: Press asks about the late Justice Tenny
jokes.punchline: The tribute pivots from 'friend to all people' to the Citizens United corporate personhood doctrine as if it's a warm personal quality
jokes.explanation: Solid political satire — using the eulogistic framing to critique corporate personhood doctrine. The structure is elegant and the joke is genuinely smart, though it lands as a familiar political target rather than a fresh observation.

jokes.text: No, no, no, no, no, there is absolutely no validity to the rumor that the president is being considered for the seat. / Do you mean President Meyer?
jokes.setup: Reporter asks about the Supreme Court vacancy and who Montez is considering
jokes.punchline: Mike reflexively denies a rumor that doesn't even exist yet, effectively creating and then denying it simultaneously
jokes.explanation: Exceptional structural comedy. Mike's Pavlovian denial instinct is so strong he invents a rumor while denying it. The reporter's bewildered 'Do you mean President Meyer? Is that even a rumor?' completes the circuit perfectly. Deep character integration — Mike's defining trait (ineptitude masquerading as spin) expressed in one action.

jokes.text: I just habitually deny everything these days. It gets me in trouble with Wendy, too. The other day, I flushed a comb down the toilet.
jokes.setup: Selina demands to know what just happened with the press
jokes.punchline: Mike explains his denial habit extends to domestic life, with the comb-down-toilet detail being a surreally mundane non-sequitur
jokes.explanation: The comb-down-the-toilet detail is brilliantly specific and absurd — it's such a weird thing to deny that it implies a rich, chaotic domestic life. Strong character work extending Mike's PR failure into his marriage.

jokes.text: You know what I don't need is examples. / I do wanna hear the end of that, though.
jokes.setup: Mike begins to give examples of his denial problem
jokes.punchline: Selina shuts it down; another staffer contradicts her, expressing genuine curiosity about the comb story
jokes.explanation: A quick two-beat joke — Selina's authority is immediately undercut by a staffer's genuine curiosity. The rhythm is perfect Veep ensemble work. The implicit acknowledgment that the comb story IS interesting adds a layer.

jokes.text: You think I'd make a good judge? / You do like throwing books at people.
jokes.setup: Danny offers to float Jane's name for the Supreme Court vacancy
jokes.punchline: Danny's endorsement is her habit of throwing books at people
jokes.explanation: Neat joke — the judicial qualification being her violence is satirically tight. It's also character-specific world-building (Jane throwing books is apparently an established fact). Good economy.

jokes.text: When we return, more of that signature Danny Egan wit. Plus, reading... could it be bad for your child's self-esteem? Why one Texas housewife wants to phase it out.
jokes.setup: TV show throws to commercial
jokes.punchline: The teaser for the next segment — 'could reading be bad for your child's self-esteem?' — is a parody of cable news fear-mongering, juxtaposed with calling Danny's hack commentary 'wit'
jokes.explanation: Solid media satire — the anti-reading segment is a perfect skewering of cable news panic. 'Signature Danny Egan wit' is appropriately ironic given his mediocrity. Both beats work but neither is exceptional.

jokes.text: Stevie, when we throw to commercial, I want a full fucking beaver shot with the legs. Otherwise, what is the point?
jokes.setup: Jane gives production notes after the segment
jokes.punchline: Jane's metric for success is maximum leg display — delivered with the same clinical authority as a note about graphics or sound
jokes.explanation: Jane's matter-of-fact approach to exploiting herself/her show is sharp character comedy. The specificity of 'full fucking beaver shot' as a technical television note is genuinely funny. The character integration is strong — this is distinctly Jane.

jokes.text: I'm starting to look like a diversity hire here.
jokes.setup: Danny complains about too much bronzer making him look too tan
jokes.punchline: He frames over-bronzing as making him look like a diversity hire, which is both self-deprecating and satirically pointing at TV news tokenism
jokes.explanation: Competent satirical joke about TV optics and tokenism, delivered by a character whose vanity and cynicism make it work. The 'earned vs cheap' score dips slightly because the joke's edge is somewhat blunted by its predictability.

jokes.text: If I wanted people to think that I actually still cared about them, I would've stayed in fucking politics.
jokes.setup: Danny wants to be taken seriously by Jane
jokes.punchline: He distinguishes TV from politics by noting politics at least required the performance of caring
jokes.explanation: Sharp and cynical — Danny's logic is inverted but internally consistent. The joke also perfectly characterizes Danny: he thinks the emptiness of TV is actually an upgrade from the performed emptiness of politics. Strong character integration and a quotable line.

jokes.text: I'd be careful. A couple of them have colds. / I'm sweating my balls off here.
jokes.setup: Staffer hands Selina notes on kid chefs
jokes.punchline: Warning about sick child chefs is immediately followed by Selina's completely unrelated physical complaint
jokes.explanation: A functional comedy beat — the sick kids warning is ignored in favor of Selina's own discomfort, which is character-consistent but not particularly fresh. Sets up the heart attack arc indirectly.

jokes.text: as a female, do you feel that... / Pass!
jokes.setup: Mike attempts the first memoir interview question
jokes.punchline: Selina passes the instant she hears 'as a female' — she refuses to engage with gender-framing before the question is even finished
jokes.explanation: Perfectly economical character joke. Selina's reflexive rejection of gender-framing is both a real observation about how female politicians are treated AND a joke about her own narcissism/denial. The comedic timing — cutting off after 'as a female' — is immaculate. Very Selina.

jokes.text: Madam Pardoner. / Don't mention it. Ever.
jokes.setup: Tanz greets Selina
jokes.punchline: He calls her 'Madam Pardoner' — referencing the pardon she gave him — and she shuts it down instantly with 'Don't mention it. Ever.'
jokes.explanation: The double meaning of 'Don't mention it' — both 'you're welcome' AND 'literally never say those words again' — is elegant. Perfect economy. The callback to the pardon establishes their corrupt relationship instantly and funnily.

jokes.text: Oh, good for you. The miserable anti-Semite, but a great friend of Israel.
jokes.setup: Tanz mentions he's on the board at the Nixon Library
jokes.punchline: Selina's backhanded 'good for you' followed by Tanz's enthusiastic contradiction — Nixon the anti-Semite being praised as a friend of Israel
jokes.explanation: Sharp political history joke — the Nixon contradiction is real and the punchline correctly identifies the absurdity of Tanz's position (Jewish billionaire on Nixon Library board). Selina's 'good for you' as barely concealed contempt is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: You know what helps? Bananas. The potassium.
jokes.setup: Selina casually mentions back trouble while they discuss money
jokes.punchline: Tanz pivots to unsolicited health advice about bananas and potassium — completely derailing the negotiation
jokes.explanation: Functional character beat — the banana potassium advice as negotiating tactic/distraction is mildly funny and sets up the warm compress bit. Not exceptional on its own.

jokes.text: What, no massage? / Gary, massage him. / Mm-mm. / Mm-hmm.
jokes.setup: Tanz is given a warm compress; he pushes for more
jokes.punchline: Selina orders Gary to massage Tanz; Gary's silent refusal ('mm-mm') is immediately overridden by Selina's authoritative 'mm-hmm'
jokes.explanation: The entire exchange is done in two grunts — Gary's disgusted 'mm-mm' vs Selina's decisive 'mm-hmm'. Maximum economy. Gary's humiliation at the hands of Selina's donor relations is a running thread, and the visual of Gary being forced to massage Tanz is peak cringe Veep. Rewatch bonus for the physical staging.

jokes.text: I was thinking in the ballpark of maybe five million. / That's a big ballpark. / But I think five million is swingable.
jokes.setup: The library funding ask is finally delivered mid-massage
jokes.punchline: Tanz extends the 'ballpark' metaphor with 'swingable' — both a baseball pun and a genuine negotiating beat, with Gary massaging him throughout
jokes.explanation: The wordplay is fairly obvious but the physical comedy (negotiating five million dollars during a massage) earns a rewatch bonus. 'Swingable' works as both pun and character voice. Serviceable.

jokes.text: Well, I think that I'm partially to blame for that because I let you out of one.
jokes.setup: Tanz laments declining prison occupancy
jokes.punchline: Selina cheerfully acknowledges she contributed to the problem by pardoning Tanz himself
jokes.explanation: Spectacular callback and character joke simultaneously. Selina's breezy self-implication in the very crime being discussed — while soliciting money from the beneficiary — is peak Veep moral bankruptcy played for maximum laughs. The economy is perfect: 'I let you out of one' does enormous work. One of the episode's best.

jokes.text: Yeah, we can find you a man or a woman, but it'll probably be a man if you wanna get anything done.
jokes.setup: Tanz wants someone from the Hill to protect his prison interests
jokes.punchline: Selina — the former female president — casually endorses sexism as a pragmatic consideration
jokes.explanation: Strong character joke — Selina's willingness to traffic in sexism for a donor, despite being the woman who broke the highest glass ceiling, is darkly funny and character-consistent. Her pragmatic cynicism overrides any feminist principle.

jokes.text: Right, I want you to get in touch with Ed McPherson on Judiciary 'cause he's building an infinity pool. / On it. Too late.
jokes.setup: Selina gives Richard a task
jokes.punchline: Richard's instant 'On it. Too late.' — he already completed the task before she finished explaining it
jokes.explanation: Richard Splett's hyper-competence played as deadpan comedy is a running Veep gag, and 'On it. Too late.' is a particularly sharp execution of it. The brevity and timing are excellent. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Oh, it's cnn.com. / Oh, well, nobody looks at that. / Thanks for nothing, Mike.
jokes.setup: Selina demands CNN be put on; Gary clarifies it's the website, not TV
jokes.punchline: Selina dismisses CNN's website as nobody's medium, then blames Mike for it
jokes.explanation: Functional Selina-dismissiveness joke. The media-hierarchy snobbery (TV CNN vs. web CNN) is period-specific satire. The pivot to blaming Mike is characteristically unfair and funny. Competent but not exceptional.

jokes.text: My tits feel like these Hot Pockets.
jokes.setup: Selina is complaining about heat and discomfort (actually a heart attack)
jokes.punchline: The specific, undignified comparison of a former president's chest discomfort to Hot Pockets
jokes.explanation: Delightfully undignified. The gap between Selina's self-image (president, future Supreme Court justice) and 'my tits feel like Hot Pockets' is the core engine of the joke. Rewatch bonus because in retrospect this is her describing a heart attack. Strong quotability.

jokes.text: well, ma'am, have you seen the doctor lately? Because maybe it's meno... / Hey, don't fucking say that, okay?
jokes.setup: Selina's heat complaints prompt someone to suggest menopause
jokes.punchline: Selina cuts off 'menopause' mid-word with a threat
jokes.explanation: Starts a running gag — Selina's furious denial of menopause while having symptoms that turn out to be a heart attack. The cut-off before the word is finished is excellent timing. Sets up a richer payoff later.

jokes.text: It's not that. Seriously, that is like a sexist-beyond-sexist reaction... Mike's hair reminds me of graham cracker crumbs.
jokes.setup: Selina denies menopause with a feminist argument about medical sexism
jokes.punchline: The serious critique of gendered medical dismissal is immediately undercut by her pivot to Mike's hair looking like graham cracker crumbs
jokes.explanation: The structure is perfect — Selina briefly makes a genuinely valid feminist point, then immediately torpedoes it with a petty non sequitur. The contrast between 'sexist-beyond-sexist' (legitimate critique) and 'graham cracker crumbs' (pure Selina pettiness) is quintessential Veep character comedy. The specificity of 'graham cracker crumbs' is excellent.

jokes.text: Good news is without the tampons, it's gonna free up some space in my bag. / Where are you gonna put yours?
jokes.setup: After Selina's menopause denial rant; she's sent everyone else out; Gary alone remains
jokes.punchline: Gary tries to console her about menopause with a silver lining; Selina immediately turns it into a dig about his man-bag
jokes.explanation: Exceptional joke. Gary's tender, slightly absurd attempt to find a silver lining in Selina's menopause is immediately weaponized against him. 'Where are you gonna put yours?' is a perfect Gary/Selina beat — she accepts his intimacy only to use it as ammunition. The implication about Gary carrying tampons for her is also a beautiful, wordless reveal about the depth of his servitude. Very high quotability.

jokes.text: I pardoned 'Typhoid Moishe.'
jokes.setup: Selina is on the phone defending her Sherman Tanz relationship
jokes.punchline: She refers to Tanz as 'Typhoid Moishe' — a nickname that implies both his contagious corruption and his Jewish heritage, delivered with casual intimacy
jokes.explanation: Inventive nickname — 'Typhoid Moishe' is funny because it's the kind of affectionate ethnic diminutive Selina would use, and it implies both the contagious corruption of the Typhoid Mary reference and familiarity. High originality for the coinage.

jokes.text: Meyer certainly would be a super left field choice... / Super left field? I mean, is that even a position? / Mm-hmm, that was my position in Little League. I was a pitcher's helper.
jokes.setup: TV anchor says Selina would be a 'super left field' Supreme Court choice
jokes.punchline: Selina challenges the baseball metaphor as not a real position; Richard confirms it WAS his position: 'pitcher's helper'
jokes.explanation: Richard's 'pitcher's helper' is a beautifully absurd invented baseball position that somehow makes perfect sense for his character. The joke works on two levels: Selina's legitimate point that it's not a real position, and Richard's earnest confirmation of a non-existent role he apparently played. Classic Richard deadpan.

jokes.text: This is insane, isn't it? / I know! It's fucking absurd! / Well, you don't have to agree with me that strongly.
jokes.setup: Selina says the Supreme Court rumor is insane, expecting agreement that it's impossible
jokes.punchline: Richard enthusiastically agrees it's absurd — but Selina realizes she wanted validation, not agreement
jokes.explanation: Classic Selina ego trap — she was fishing for 'insane but maybe possible!' and got 'yes, completely insane.' Richard's oblivious sincerity makes it land harder. The structure is clean: setup, enthusiastic misread, perfect callback-reversal.

jokes.text: Well, I suspect that this may be myocardial ischemia... I think you had a small heart attack.
jokes.setup: The entire scene has been played as Selina potentially having menopause
jokes.punchline: The reveal: she's actually having a heart attack — the 'menopause' running gag pays off with something far more serious
jokes.explanation: Structurally excellent — the entire menopause running gag pays off with a genuine medical crisis. The joke is that Selina was RIGHT that it wasn't menopause (giving her a twisted vindication) while also being in much more danger than anyone feared. Huge rewatch bonus as every prior symptom complaint gets recontextualized.

jokes.text: So, I'm not menopausal? / Well, no, but this is far more serious. / Well, hand me a cracker and spread it up with 'I Can't Believe It's Not Menopause.'
jokes.setup: Doctor tells Selina she had a heart attack, which is far more serious than menopause
jokes.punchline: Selina's first response to learning she had a heart attack is relief about not being menopausal, celebrated with a butter substitute pun
jokes.explanation: One of the best jokes in the episode and possibly the series. The structure is flawless: Selina's obsession with not being menopausal is so intense she experiences a heart attack diagnosis as GOOD NEWS. The 'I Can't Believe It's Not Menopause' pun is executed perfectly — it lands as both a clever wordplay riff AND a profound character revelation. Only Selina Meyer would coin this phrase at this exact moment. Exceptional on every dimension. Genuinely 8+ territory — comparable to the anchor examples.

jokes.text: You wanna knock me up, Doc? Just 'cause I can do it.
jokes.setup: Doctor says they need to get her to a hospital
jokes.punchline: Selina mishears/misprocesses the urgency and pivots to a joke about her reproductive viability
jokes.explanation: Selina's breezy deflection of medical crisis with a sexual quip is in character, but the joke itself is a bit loose. The implication ('just 'cause I can do it' as a flex) is funny but the structure is weaker than surrounding jokes.

jokes.text: It's just a heart attack! Gary! Gar...
jokes.setup: Doctor insists they need to get to a hospital immediately
jokes.punchline: 'It's just a heart attack!' — Selina's breezy dismissiveness applied to a cardiac event, followed by her immediately collapsing/needing Gary
jokes.explanation: 'It's just a heart attack' is a glorious piece of Selina understatement — her dismissiveness of inconvenient reality extended to a life-threatening event. The immediate call for Gary (whose first instinct is always Selina) while she's supposedly dismissing the severity is perfect. Very quotable.

jokes.text: Maybe my uterus is just wonky. / No, Catherine. It's porcelain and perfect.
jokes.setup: Catherine worries about her mother's health during the fertility subplot
jokes.punchline: Selina defends her uterus as 'porcelain and perfect' while en route to the hospital with a heart attack
jokes.explanation: Selina defending her uterus's aesthetic quality while having a heart attack is beautifully absurd. 'Porcelain and perfect' is such a specific, vain, and funny way to describe an internal organ. High character integration — only Selina.

jokes.text: You know what I like about you two? It's not clear who's the top. / I am.
jokes.setup: Catherine and Marjorie are at a fertility clinic; the doctor makes an observation
jokes.punchline: Doctor's observation about their relationship dynamic is immediately and flatly corrected by Marjorie
jokes.explanation: Marjorie's blunt, immediate correction is perfectly in character for her military-precision personality. The doctor's inappropriate observation is a setup for her flat dominance assertion. Economy is excellent.

jokes.text: Why don't we just puppy pile, okay? Then we'll all get a bite to eat afterwards, you know. See a movie or something. You know, we'll make it nice.
jokes.setup: The doctor suggests the donor (Gary's brother/friend) have direct sexual intercourse with Catherine to increase conception chances
jokes.punchline: The doctor pivots to suggesting a 'puppy pile' and then a movie date — making the threesome proposition sound like a casual hangout plan
jokes.explanation: The casual domestication of a highly clinical and awkward threesome proposition — 'we'll make it nice' — is absurdly funny. The doctor's breezy social planning (movie, dinner) makes the sexual suggestion feel even stranger.

jokes.text: He does have very delicate features. / Thank you.
jokes.setup: The women are considering whether to pursue the direct intercourse option with the donor
jokes.punchline: The donor accepts 'very delicate features' as a compliment with complete earnestness
jokes.explanation: Functional joke — the donor's vanity accepting a backhanded compliment. Competent but not remarkable.

jokes.text: Sure, whatever. I'm just the cream filling in this gay-claire.
jokes.setup: Doctor asks if it's okay to discuss results in front of everyone
jokes.punchline: Dan describes his position in the lesbian couple's fertility situation as 'the cream filling in a gay-claire' (éclair)
jokes.explanation: Genuinely exceptional wordplay. 'Gay-claire' (éclair) is a perfectly constructed pun — the éclair metaphor for his role as sperm donor between two women is both anatomically apt and brilliantly coined. It's the kind of pun that requires you to hear it twice to fully appreciate. High originality, the term is entirely novel. 'Cream filling' does double duty. This is legitimately close to the 8.0 anchor in cleverness and quotability, possibly above it.

jokes.text: Seriously? / I'm so sorry, Dan. / So, I've been pulling out this entire time for nothing?
jokes.setup: Doctor delivers news that Dan's sperm motility is too low to conceive
jokes.punchline: Dan's first response to learning he's infertile is concern about wasted contraceptive effort
jokes.explanation: One of the best character jokes in the episode. Dan's complete narcissism — reframing infertility as a personal inconvenience about birth control — is pitch perfect. The structure is immaculate: sympathy extended, then immediately subverted by Dan's self-centeredness. 'Pulling out this entire time for nothing' is both logically coherent (from Dan's perspective) and hilariously wrong-headed.

jokes.text: Oh, I am gonna save a fortune in morning-after pills. There are, like, three girls that owe me an abortion refund.
jokes.setup: Dan continues processing his infertility news
jokes.punchline: He escalates from birth control math to demanding retroactive abortion refunds from past partners
jokes.explanation: Escalation done brilliantly. The pull-out joke was already shocking; demanding an 'abortion refund' is the escalation that makes both jokes better. The specificity of 'three girls' makes it concrete and funnier. 'Abortion refund' as a concept is both appalling and a perfect window into Dan's transactional, sociopathic character. This is Veep at its darkest and sharpest.

jokes.text: Ladies, I am Danny Egan, and I will see you in the tomorrow.
jokes.setup: Dan processes infertility as liberation rather than bad news and prepares to exit
jokes.punchline: 'I will see you in the tomorrow' — a grandiose, slightly mangled phrase that positions Dan as a new man reborn through infertility
jokes.explanation: The grammatically incorrect 'the tomorrow' as a pompous Dan-ism is excellent. It's him at his most delusionally self-aggrandizing — announcing himself by full name to the two women he just tried to impregnate. Classic Dan exit energy.

jokes.text: Oh, my God, I can't believe I just did that. Um, uh, so we should go to Washington probably, I guess. / I guess.
jokes.setup: Catherine receives the news that her mother had a heart attack
jokes.punchline: After an extended fertility scene, Catherine's response to her mother's heart attack is maximum uncertainty ('probably, I guess') and Marjorie's flat one-word mirror ('I guess')
jokes.explanation: The comedy is in the profound ambivalence — Catherine genuinely doesn't know if she should go to her own mother's heart attack. Marjorie's 'I guess' as confirmation of maximum doubt is perfectly deadpan. Strong character integration (this is a very Catherine/Marjorie beat) with excellent economy.

jokes.text: Did I die? / Here we go again. / Is this heaven? / Yeah, he keeps waking up and asking that.
jokes.setup: Gary wakes up after his heart attack
jokes.punchline: This is apparently not his first time waking up asking if he's dead — 'here we go again' and 'he keeps waking up and asking that'
jokes.explanation: The joke is Gary's repeated death-questioning being a logistical inconvenience to medical staff. 'Here we go again' as the exasperated medical response is funny. Strong Gary character integration — his childlike wonder even in cardiac crisis is very on-brand.

jokes.text: No, I had a heart episode and they're just gonna pop a stent in there just for fun.
jokes.setup: Gary asks if Selina also had a heart attack
jokes.punchline: Selina reframes her heart attack as a 'heart episode' and the stent as being done 'just for fun'
jokes.explanation: Selina's diminishing language ('heart episode', 'just for fun') applied to cardiac surgery is excellent deadpan. Classic her — controlling the narrative even when her heart is failing. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Can we get something for Mr. Walsh, please? Because he's the one who's really ailing and at death's door.
jokes.setup: Selina and Gary are both hospitalized; Selina is seen as the more serious case
jokes.punchline: Selina redirects medical attention to Gary as if he's the priority, when in fact she's doing this to downplay her own condition
jokes.explanation: Selina using Gary's condition to minimize her own is characteristically manipulative. The joke works on the irony that she actually IS the more serious patient. Functional character beat.

jokes.text: Am I at death's door? / Yes, Madam President. / Oh, I'm at death's door?
jokes.setup: Selina tells Gary he's at death's door as a cover story
jokes.punchline: Gary apparently didn't know this about his own condition and is alarmed to hear it from Selina
jokes.explanation: Gary finding out from Selina (who is using it as spin) that he's apparently at death's door is a solid character beat. His trusting confusion is very Gary — he accepts Selina's framing of his own medical condition.

jokes.text: Just think... next year, you get to go to my library opening. / And you get to buy my book. / Can't you just give me a copy? / Well, I can't be giving out free copies of the book 'cause think of the position that that would put me in.
jokes.setup: Selina is trying to give Gary reasons to live
jokes.punchline: Her reasons for Gary to live are entirely self-promotional activities; when he asks for a free book copy she calculates the commercial implications while he's in a hospital bed
jokes.explanation: Exceptional character comedy — Selina's 'reasons to live' speech for Gary is entirely comprised of Selina's own promotional needs. When Gary asks for a free book, she seriously contemplates the commercial implications. The depth of her self-absorption is horrifying and hilarious. The 'think of the position that would put me in' line while he's in hospital is peak Selina. Strong candidate for best character joke in the episode.

jokes.text: And you're gonna have a birthday next... / August. / ...August. Good for you. That's right. And you're gonna be... / 40. / ...40. / You remembered. / Yeah. You're only 40?
jokes.setup: Selina continues listing reasons for Gary to live, specifically his upcoming birthday
jokes.punchline: She doesn't remember Gary's birthday month or age; when he supplies both, she ends with 'You're only 40?' — mild surprise that undercuts the entire supportive speech
jokes.explanation: The structure — Gary having to supply all the details of his own birthday to Selina who then claims to 'remember' — builds beautifully. 'You're only 40?' as the payoff is the perfect note of mild surprise that demolishes any illusion of their relationship being reciprocal. She doesn't even know how old Gary is. One of the episode's best sustained cringe-comedy beats.

jokes.text: And I'm gonna... I'm gonna have a party. / Oh, really? / In Alabama.
jokes.setup: Selina promises Gary a birthday party
jokes.punchline: The party will be in Alabama — a seemingly deliberate geographic punishment
jokes.explanation: A perfect one-word punchline. The beat — 'Oh, really?' setting up genuine hope — followed by 'In Alabama' is timed impeccably. Alabama as the location is funny because of everything it implies about Selina's opinion of Gary's worth: she'll throw him a party, but in the most inconvenient, undesirable location. Maximum economy, strong character integration.

jokes.text: If they're wondering why I'm at the hospital, you just put that on Gary 'cause he had a massive heart attack, luckily.
jokes.setup: Selina instructs staff to maintain her closed-door meeting cover story
jokes.punchline: 'Luckily' — Gary's massive heart attack is characterized as fortunate cover for Selina's own cardiac event
jokes.explanation: 'Luckily' is the single funniest word in this sentence. Selina's transactional relationship with Gary's near-death experience as a PR asset is quintessential Veep. Maximum economy — one adverb encapsulates the entire moral horror of the Selina-Gary relationship. High quotability.

jokes.text: 'Cause I don't know the alarm code to get back into my house.
jokes.setup: Selina adds to the reasons Gary must survive
jokes.punchline: After the library opening, the book, the Alabama birthday — the final reason Gary must live is that Selina doesn't know her own alarm code
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback to cap the reasons-to-live sequence. After the self-promotional speeches, the 'real' reason Gary must survive is Selina's helplessness with her own security system. It reframes the entire preceding scene as motivated by Selina's practical dependence. Strong economy and character integration.

jokes.text: Just give him that whale dong Jonah, my God.
jokes.setup: Staff can't find anyone in the Senate to represent Tanz's prison interests
jokes.punchline: Selina suggests Jonah Ryan, identifying him only by his anatomical nickname
jokes.explanation: Selina reducing Jonah to 'whale dong' as his primary identifier is funny character comedy. The casualness of it — suggesting him as a political asset while using only his nickname — is very Selina. Functional but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Well, well, well. If it isn't the prodigal traitor come back home to suck on my giant congressional ball. / No, it's Richard Splett.
jokes.setup: Jonah answers a call expecting it to be from someone specific
jokes.punchline: His prepared aggressive greeting ('prodigal traitor...giant congressional ball') is entirely deflated by 'No, it's Richard Splett'
jokes.explanation: The joke works on multiple levels: Jonah has a pre-written insult monologue for an anticipated caller, only for it to land on completely unintimidating Richard Splett. 'No, it's Richard Splett' is one of the best straight-man lines in the episode — the name itself is funny, the flatness is perfect, and it completely deflates Jonah's self-important aggression. Strong character work.

jokes.text: Well, well, well. If it isn't the prodigal ex-girlfriend come home to suck on my giant... / Listen, jizz clot...
jokes.setup: Jonah takes a second call immediately after, reprising the same greeting formula
jokes.punchline: He begins the same 'prodigal...suck on my giant' pattern for a different caller, before being cut off by Selina's 'jizz clot'
jokes.explanation: The callback to the same speech pattern seconds later — now re-templated for a different caller ('ex-girlfriend') — is both a running gag and a character revelation: Jonah has pre-prepared aggressive greetings for everyone. 'Jizz clot' cutting him off is a great counterpunch. Selina's insults are consistently the best in Veep.

jokes.text: Private prison and pay day lending magnate, Israel hyper-enthusiast, billionaire, convicted of tax fraud. Yeah, when people think about what's wrong in Washington, they think of him and you.
jokes.setup: Jonah asks his staff who Sherman Tanz is
jokes.punchline: After a litany of Tanz's crimes and associations, the kicker is '...they think of him and you' — placing Jonah in equivalent moral territory
jokes.explanation: The list of Tanz's credentials building to Jonah being in the same category is a solid setup/punchline. 'Israel hyper-enthusiast' as a descriptor is particularly good — the specificity of 'hyper' is funny. The payoff could be sharper but it lands.

jokes.text: Association with Tanz spells disaster. / So, I shouldn't meet with him? / Well, for you, Congressman, disaster would be a step up.
jokes.setup: Staff warns Jonah that Tanz is toxic
jokes.punchline: When Jonah asks if he shouldn't meet with him, the response is that disaster would be an improvement for Jonah specifically
jokes.explanation: Clean, well-structured insult joke. The pivot — first establishing Tanz as a disaster, then revealing Jonah is below disaster — is a satisfying two-step. Good economy. Typical high-quality Veep put-down.

jokes.text: Apparently, someone saw President Meyer's name on McPherson's call sheet, and he's the chair of the Judiciary Committee. / I'm sorry, could you repeat everything after 'Hey, buddy'? / Oh, shit, hold on one sec.
jokes.setup: Mike is trying to do damage control about the Supreme Court rumor with Richard on hold
jokes.punchline: Richard, who has been on hold the whole time, asks Mike to repeat everything from 'Hey, buddy' — and Mike abandons the entire conversation for something else
jokes.explanation: Functional comedy of bureaucratic chaos — Richard has been listening on hold to sensitive political information and now politely asks for a recap. The timing of Mike abandoning him again is good. Competent Veep ensemble work.

jokes.text: Chocolate chip walnut cookies from Levain. / Mm-hmm. / You're lucky I have such a great ass.
jokes.setup: Mike approaches Jane with cookies during a sensitive conversation
jokes.punchline: Jane accepts the cookies with the observation that Mike is 'lucky she has a great ass' — implying the cookies are meant to flatter/appease her vanity
jokes.explanation: Jane's self-congratulatory non sequitur is a good character moment — she interprets cookie-bribery as commentary on her physical appearance. The logic is Jane-specific and funny. Competent.

jokes.text: All I can tell you is that President Meyer is in a closed-door meeting. / Wait, so this Supreme Court shit's real? / All I can tell you is President Meyer is in a closed-door...
jokes.setup: Richard is stonewalling Mike about Selina's whereabouts
jokes.punchline: His 'closed-door meeting' deflection is so robotic that Mike immediately recognizes it as confirmation, and Richard just keeps repeating it
jokes.explanation: The joke is that the non-denial denial is so obvious it's practically confirmation. Richard's glitchy repetition of the script after being called out is funny. Competent but familiar political comedy trope.

jokes.text: Hey, guys, I have insider confirmation that Selina Meyer is being considered for the open Supreme Court seat. / To what, reupholster it?
jokes.setup: Mike announces his Supreme Court scoop
jokes.punchline: 'To what, reupholster it?' — Mike's scoop is met with a joke questioning Selina's qualification as furniture rather than a justice
jokes.explanation: 'To what, reupholster it?' is an elegant, economic insult — it questions Selina's qualifications while also mocking the idea with a single verb. Sharp and funny. Good economy.

jokes.text: Oh, also, can we do something about my makeup girl and her Hinckley-esque obsession with bronzer? / She's not going anywhere. She's the head makeup artist's niece. / I'm starting to look like I should be auditioning for a telenovela here.
jokes.setup: Danny tries to use his new 'Jane's man' status to fix the bronzer problem
jokes.punchline: Jane shuts him down (nepotism); Danny escalates the complaint with 'auditioning for a telenovela'
jokes.explanation: Callback to the bronzer issue with escalation. 'Hinckley-esque' is a funny qualifier — obsessive, potentially dangerous bronzer application. The telenovela line is a solid callback payoff. Competent running gag work.

jokes.text: Come on, I haven't slept with a woman over 30 since I was 14, and that's 'cause I needed the grade.
jokes.setup: Colleagues accuse Danny of sleeping with Jane
jokes.punchline: His denial is that he hasn't slept with a woman over 30 since he was 14 — when he did it for grades
jokes.explanation: Exceptional character joke. The structure is brilliant — his denial of Jane is undermined by him confirming he HAS slept with older women when it was academically advantageous. The specific age of 14 makes it darker and funnier. This is deeply character-specific Danny Egan: sociopathic, transactional, and delivered with complete confidence. One of the episode's best.

jokes.text: Bronzina's fired. You're welcome.
jokes.setup: Danny can't get the bronzer artist fired through Jane
jokes.punchline: He apparently fires her unilaterally and nicknames her 'Bronzina' — presenting this as a gift to colleagues
jokes.explanation: The nickname 'Bronzina' is good, and the unilateral firing presented as a favor is character-consistent Danny overreach. Functional callback resolution.

jokes.text: Oh! You got me some bodega flowers?
jokes.setup: Catherine and Marjorie arrive at the hospital with flowers
jokes.punchline: Selina's first assessment is that they're cheap bodega flowers — not gratitude for the gesture
jokes.explanation: Selina's immediate evaluation of the flowers as cheap is characteristically ungracious. 'Bodega flowers' as a category is a nice specific touch. Functional character beat.

jokes.text: They're actually from a very expensive boutique. / Oh, okay, well, I like them, wherever they're from. / We can just give them to Gary.
jokes.setup: Selina is corrected about the flowers being expensive
jokes.punchline: Selina offers tepid appreciation; Marjorie immediately suggests giving the expensive flowers to Gary — dismissing the gesture entirely
jokes.explanation: Marjorie's flat redirect — suggesting the expensive flowers go to Gary without ceremony — is perfectly Marjorie: efficient, unsentimental, somehow both dismissive and pragmatic. Good character integration.

jokes.text: Whoo, that Montez is a Latin piece of ass. / Okay, he's hallucinating. Come here, come here, come out here.
jokes.setup: Gary watches Montez on TV while heavily medicated
jokes.punchline: Gary — loyal, devoted Gary — makes a lustful comment about Montez on heavy medication; Selina immediately attributes it to hallucination
jokes.explanation: Gary admiring Montez (of all people) while medicated is very funny because it violates everything we know about his singular devotion to Selina. Selina's instant 'he's hallucinating' is both medically wrong and revealing — she can't process Gary having thoughts about anyone else. Great character beat.

jokes.text: Gary Walsh? / Yeah. / If you will just sign here, please. / 'Yas,' queen.
jokes.setup: Nurse asks Gary to sign discharge papers
jokes.punchline: Gary responds to the nurse's professional 'if you will sign here please' with 'Yas, queen'
jokes.explanation: Gary's 'yas queen' to the nurse is a mild character moment — post-cardiac Gary is clearly still loopy. It's sweet and slightly funny but not particularly original.

jokes.text: Do you have someone at home who can take care of you? / Yes, my roommates Stashelle, Lark, and Boosh. / You have three roommates? / We share a studio in Hoboken.
jokes.setup: Nurse asks about Gary's post-hospital care arrangements
jokes.punchline: The revelation that Gary — personal assistant to a former president — lives in a studio in Hoboken with three roommates named Stashelle, Lark, and Boosh
jokes.explanation: One of the best jokes in the episode. The gap between Gary's role (quasi-personal companion to the president) and his reality (studio apartment, Hoboken, three roommates with names like Stashelle, Lark, and Boosh) is perfectly calibrated cringe comedy. The names alone ('Boosh' especially) are inspired. It recontextualizes Gary's entire existence as even more pathetic than we thought. Very high quotability and rewatch value — this is a major character revelation delivered as a throwaway.

jokes.text: Your mom doesn't think you're pretty, but I think you're stunning.
jokes.setup: Gary is still loopy on medication after his heart attack
jokes.punchline: Gary spontaneously reassures the nurse about her self-esteem with a backhanded compliment about her mother
jokes.explanation: Post-cardiac Gary is apparently dispensing unsolicited, oddly specific emotional support to strangers. 'Your mom doesn't think you're pretty, but I think you're stunning' is both completely random and a somehow touching Gary non sequitur. The specificity of 'your mom' makes it surreal. High quotability.

jokes.text: Yeah, and what's the first thing you remember about him? / He got stuck in a bathtub? / No, well, the second thing you remember about him. / He was buried in a piano case.
jokes.setup: Selina is arguing that like Taft, a president can serve on the Supreme Court
jokes.punchline: The 'second thing' about Taft (after the bathtub) is apparently being buried in a piano case — Gary's confused historical knowledge is both wrong and specific
jokes.explanation: Gary's 'piano case' is a beautiful non-answer — wrong enough to be funny, specific enough to be a real guess. The structure (Selina fishing for 'served on the Supreme Court,' Gary giving the bathtub, then the piano case) is an escalating absurdist miscommunication. 'Piano case' alone is a great piece of comedy writing.

jokes.text: Ooh, Monday, it's the day after Daylight Saving Time. It's my favorite holiday 'cause it's like living in a tiny version of 'Back to the Future.'
jokes.setup: Richard is told to schedule a Monday meeting
jokes.punchline: Richard treats Daylight Saving Time as a 'favorite holiday' and compares it to Back to the Future, completely derailing the conversation
jokes.explanation: Richard treating DST as a holiday and the 'Back to the Future' analogy is perfectly Richard — enthusiastic, sincere, completely inappropriate timing. This also sets up the episode's Daylight Saving Time running gag. 'A tiny version of Back to the Future' is an endearingly specific description. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: He can't go all the way back to Hoboken. / What? What are you talking about? You live in New Jersey? / You're at the brownstone by 6:00. I leave a little after 4:00, I catch the 4:28 PATH... / Okay. / ...then I take the F to the 6, and then I do a quick jaunt, and I'm there in time to steep the tea...
jokes.setup: Gary's Hoboken living situation is revealed; discussion of his recovery care
jokes.punchline: Gary's extremely detailed commute description — delivered while in a hospital bed after a heart attack — includes arriving in time 'to steep the tea' for Selina
jokes.explanation: Devastating character comedy. Gary's detailed commute planning — from a hospital bed, after a heart attack, still calculating how to be at Selina's service — is the ultimate expression of his psychological servitude. 'In time to steep the tea' as the endpoint of his elaborate PATH-and-subway journey is the perfect note. Rewatch bonus and very strong character integration.

jokes.text: No, my legacy, guys, is me in a robe, taking guns from this guy, giving the death penalty to that guy. And they can't vote me out, right? It's for life.
jokes.setup: Selina is told a library is still good for her
jokes.punchline: Selina's vision of being a Supreme Court justice — 'taking guns from this guy, giving the death penalty to that guy' — reduces constitutional jurisprudence to petty personal power trips
jokes.explanation: Excellent Selina character joke — her conception of Supreme Court power is entirely transactional and petty ('this guy', 'that guy'). The real punchline is 'they can't vote me out, right? It's for life' — revealing that her entire interest in the Court is its unaccountability. Deeply character-specific and satirically on point.

jokes.text: They're gonna have to carry me out of there with the gavel clenched in my cold, dead twat.
jokes.setup: Selina doubles down on her lifetime appointment enthusiasm
jokes.punchline: The graphic, profane image of dying in the Supreme Court — 'gavel clenched in my cold, dead twat'
jokes.explanation: One of the best lines in the episode. 'Gavel clenched in my cold, dead twat' is a virtuoso piece of comedy writing — it simultaneously parodies 'cold dead hands' gun rhetoric, uses profanity in an unexpected regal context, and is a deeply character-specific statement of Selina's ambition. The combination of Supreme Court dignity and anatomical profanity is perfect. Extremely high quotability. This approaches the anchor example level.

jokes.text: Oh, Gary, I need you to move all of these law review boxes. / Love to, yeah. / Ow! / See, now it hurts for me to point. That muscle's called the brachioradialis. At least it is in cats.
jokes.setup: Selina asks Gary (just discharged from the hospital after a heart attack) to move boxes
jokes.punchline: Richard identifies the muscle Selina hurt while pointing — but qualifies it: 'at least it is in cats'
jokes.explanation: Richard's anatomical qualification — confidently naming the muscle then undermining it with 'at least it is in cats' — is absurdist comedy gold. The 'brachioradialis' specificity followed by 'in cats' is an inspired piece of writing. Also: Selina asking heart-attack Gary to move boxes is its own joke, and the irony of Selina's own post-cardiac complaint upstaging it is perfectly layered.

jokes.text: the Judiciary Committee has asked to send them everything that you've ever written on abortion. / Well, I can give them my actual abortion if I could find it lying around here somewhere. / I'll check the freezer.
jokes.setup: Judiciary Committee wants Selina's writings on abortion for Supreme Court vetting
jokes.punchline: Selina offers to provide her 'actual abortion'; Gary offers to check the freezer for it
jokes.explanation: Exceptional two-beat joke. Selina's 'actual abortion' offer is shockingly blunt and funny; Gary's 'I'll check the freezer' is the perfect completion — his unquestioning servitude applied to an impossible task, delivered with complete sincerity. The freezer detail implies Gary has a filing system that might include this. Extremely quotable and deeply character-specific.

jokes.text: Well, I can give them my actual abortion if I could find it lying around here somewhere. / I'll check the freezer.
jokes.setup: The Judiciary Committee wants Selina's writings on abortion
jokes.punchline: Selina offering 'her actual abortion' as a submission; Gary's practical 'I'll check the freezer'
jokes.explanation: The two-part structure is excellent: Selina's morbid/absurd offer + Gary's completely literal practical response. 'I'll check the freezer' is Gary at his best — not horrified, not amused, just functionally addressing the request. The dark subject matter is handled with the show's characteristic breezy amorality. Justifies 8.5.

jokes.text: If I'm going to be a Supreme, I don't really think we need to finish this book.
jokes.setup: Mike suggests the Supreme Court angle is a great twist ending for the memoir
jokes.punchline: Selina immediately cancels the book project she's been working on all episode, reasoning that a Supreme doesn't need a memoir
jokes.explanation: Selina's instant abandonment of the memoir project — her 'legacy' project — the moment something shinier appears is great character comedy. Her logic is sound within Selina's value system. Functional character beat.

jokes.text: I'm sorry, Mama. Bunny's late for middle school. I'll see you later.
jokes.setup: Catherine is visiting her mother after a heart attack
jokes.punchline: Catherine inexplicably refers to herself as 'Bunny' and claims she's late for middle school — a non sequitur that positions Catherine as both childlike and completely unmoored
jokes.explanation: Catherine calling herself 'Bunny' and claiming to be late for middle school is wonderfully absurd — it captures her perpetual emotional regression around Selina perfectly. The non sequitur is specific enough to be funny and character-revealing. Good economy.

jokes.text: Tell me what gets this Tanz guy's dick hard. / Being on time, Congressman Dachau. You're an hour late. He's been sitting in your office the whole time.
jokes.setup: Jonah arrives for his Tanz meeting in a power-play mode
jokes.punchline: His confident question about Tanz's motivations is met with the information that his own unreliability is the answer, plus 'Congressman Dachau'
jokes.explanation: 'Congressman Dachau' as a casual epithet for Jonah is a devastating one-word insult (implying he is himself a human atrocity). The joke also perfectly deflates his power-walk entrance — he was trying to seem in control, and he's actually just late. Strong structure.

jokes.text: Do I need to timesplain clock reading to you two? The big hand points to the noon and the little hand points to the... Well, fuck, it's the... God, it's 10:00 AM.
jokes.setup: Jonah challenges his staff about being an hour late
jokes.punchline: He begins to 'timesplain' how clocks work, then realizes mid-mansplain that he's actually reading the clock wrong himself
jokes.explanation: Exceptional joke with a perfect structure — Jonah's condescension ('timesplain') sets him up for his own humiliation in real time. He begins explaining clock reading to his staff, then discovers he can't do it himself. The moment of realization ('Well, fuck, it's the... God, it's 10:00 AM') is perfectly written. The coinage of 'timesplain' is also inspired. One of the episode's best.

jokes.text: You didn't set your watch forward. / Happy Daylight Savings Time. / Saving. / Whatever. / Oh, shit, Daylight Savings Time is today? / Saving, and it started yesterday.
jokes.setup: The reason for Jonah's lateness is revealed
jokes.punchline: Jonah doesn't know the correct name of the holiday ('savings' vs 'saving'), and also didn't know it happened
jokes.explanation: Establishes the DST running gag with Jonah. Kent's pedantic correction of 'savings' vs 'saving' is a good character beat. Functional setup for the broader DST theme of the episode.

jokes.text: Okay, but you're in... / Fine.
jokes.setup: Jonah arrives to find Tanz sitting in Jonah's own chair
jokes.punchline: Jonah's objection ('okay, but you're in...') is immediately shut down by Tanz's flat 'Fine.' — and Jonah just accepts it, sitting elsewhere in his own office
jokes.explanation: The visual of Tanz in Jonah's power chair, established by Jonah's half-objection, is a good dominance joke. The economy ('Fine' as complete shutdown) is excellent. Rewatch bonus for the staging.

jokes.text: I like to judge a man by looking him right in the eyes.
jokes.setup: Tanz positions himself as someone who reads people by eye contact
jokes.punchline: The following beat (a pause) implies that he's staring intensely at Jonah — who, given his general weirdness, is presumably very uncomfortable being stared at
jokes.explanation: The setup relies heavily on the visual of Tanz and Jonah's extended eye contact stare. On page it's a cringe setup; on screen it would play as an uncomfortable pause. Rewatch bonus for the visual.

jokes.text: Are you gonna make a campaign contribution or what? / You know what? I am. / Getting paid, getting laid, son.
jokes.setup: After the extended staring contest, Jonah cuts to the chase
jokes.punchline: Tanz agrees to donate; Jonah celebrates with 'Getting paid, getting laid, son' — an embarrassing flex
jokes.explanation: Jonah's trashy celebration of a donation with 'Getting paid, getting laid, son' is excellent character comedy — he's treating a corrupt political donation like a personal win on the street. Sets up the immediate subversion.

jokes.text: Congressman Jonah Ryan. Zero dollars and one cent.
jokes.setup: Jonah is celebrating Tanz's agreement to donate; the check is produced
jokes.punchline: The check is for one cent — Tanz sat in his office for an hour just to give him literally nothing
jokes.explanation: Devastating punchline to the entire Tanz-Jonah sequence. The one cent check is the perfect comedic payoff — it retroactively makes every moment of Jonah's posturing and 'getting paid' celebration into humiliation. Tanz waited an hour just to perform this contempt. Excellent structure and economy. The specific '$0.01' is an inspired choice — not $1, not $0 — the minimum possible non-zero insult.

jokes.text: Did you have a stroke, you weird asshole man? You stuck around here for an hour just to be a dick? / I did. / You didn't even sign this. / Maybe you should've apologized.
jokes.setup: Jonah reacts to the one-cent check
jokes.punchline: Tanz confirms he wasted an hour for this; the unsigned check makes it even more worthless; and the kicker: 'maybe you should've apologized'
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback — Tanz told Jonah never to apologize, and now reveals the entire exercise was to punish Jonah for being late. The unsigned check escalating the worthlessness, and 'maybe you should've apologized' as the moral, is a perfect three-part escalation. 'You weird asshole man' is also a great Jonah compound insult.

jokes.text: Ding-dong! Dinner is served! What? / You should not be in the maid's quarters.
jokes.setup: Gary has moved into Selina's brownstone; he appears with dinner
jokes.punchline: He's cooking from the 'maid's quarters' — he's been given the servant's room. His cheery 'ding-dong!' entrance contrasts with the indignity of his accommodation.
jokes.explanation: The 'maid's quarters' revelation is a quiet character joke — Gary moved into Selina's house to recover from a heart attack and was given the servant's room. His cheerful obliviousness to this indignity is very Gary.

jokes.text: Oh, I cut up a chicken sandwich and I put it into pasta. / What? / Is this the chicken sandwich that was in the fridge before the hospital? / Wow, and you left the bun on it when you cut it.
jokes.setup: Gary presents the dinner he made while recovering from a heart attack
jokes.punchline: It's a pre-hospital chicken sandwich cut up into pasta — with the bun still on
jokes.explanation: The escalating horror of the dish — chicken sandwich (fine), into pasta (odd), from before the hospital (concerning), with the bun still on (catastrophic) — is excellently structured. Catherine's observation about the bun is a perfect additional beat. Gary's culinary effort as a post-cardiac patient is also darkly sweet.

jokes.text: Smells sort of funky, though, no? / It's the bleu cheese. / No, but there isn't any bleu cheese in it.
jokes.setup: Selina smells something off in Gary's pasta dish
jokes.punchline: Gary attributes it to bleu cheese; Selina points out there is no bleu cheese in the dish — the smell is unexplained and therefore worse
jokes.explanation: Gary's bleu cheese explanation is funny in itself; Selina's 'no, but there isn't any bleu cheese in it' is the perfect topper — the mystery funky smell is now even more alarming. The economy is excellent and the escalation of the dish's horrors continues beautifully.

jokes.text: You must be... starving yourself, though. / Oh, no, I ate. / You did? / Yeah, a chef made me some grilled salmon and some asparagus with a béarnaise sauce. It was heavenly. It was really delicious. / Okay. / I mean, I couldn't finish it. I just threw the rest out.
jokes.setup: Gary fishing to see if Selina wants more of his terrible pasta; she reveals she already ate
jokes.punchline: Selina ate a gourmet meal while Gary cooked an unsanitary pasta dish for her; she also threw out the leftovers — maximizing the contrast of their situations
jokes.explanation: The escalation — salmon, asparagus, béarnaise, 'heavenly,' and then 'I threw the rest out' — while Gary has been preparing a questionable pasta is exceptional cringe comedy. The 'I couldn't finish it. I just threw the rest out' while Gary sits across from his sad sandwich-pasta is devastating. This whole sequence is a sustained masterpiece of the Gary-Selina dynamic.

jokes.text: Where is your water? / Ugh, I don't have any. There's just bottles in the kitchen. / Oh. / Yeah, I'll go. / No, no, no, I'll go. / No, no, no, I'm gonna go, really. / Okay? I'm just... I just wanna wait a minute. / Okay. That's really sweet.
jokes.setup: Selina needs water; Gary and Selina both offer to get it
jokes.punchline: Gary — recovering from a heart attack, Selina's guest — insists on getting his hostess's water; Selina ultimately lets him; the moment is played as sweetly touching but is actually deeply wrong
jokes.explanation: A perfect tonal joke — played as a tender, almost romantic moment of mutual consideration, but Gary is literally recovering from a heart attack and should not be getting anyone's water. Selina's 'That's really sweet' accepting his service is the horror. The restraint of playing it straight rather than commenting on it makes it funnier and sadder. Strong rewatch bonus for the tonal contradiction.

jokes.text: Apparently, the state senator was against gay marriage and paying his gay escort. / We'll be right back.
jokes.setup: TV news teases a story as Gary sits alone watching
jokes.punchline: The senator was against gay marriage AND not paying his gay escort — a neatly compact hypocrisy joke
jokes.explanation: Competent political hypocrisy joke — the 'against gay marriage but had a gay escort' trope is well-worn by this point. The 'and not paying' twist is a good add but not enough to make it exceptional. Background gag that functions as set dressing.

jokes.text: Danny, I saw that piece that you did on Rwanda. It's very powerful. / You did? Fantastic! I'm so glad you like it.
jokes.setup: Jane compliments Danny's Rwanda piece
jokes.punchline: Danny's self-congratulatory response ('Fantastic! I'm so glad you like it') rather than any substantive engagement with the piece itself — he cares about the praise, not the subject
jokes.explanation: Functional Danny character beat — his narcissism making a piece about genocide all about his recognition. Works but isn't exceptional.

jokes.text: Is that what she calls you when she puts her penis in your vagina? What? What are you talking about? / We all know about you and Jane. / Come on, I haven't slept with a woman over 30 since I was 14...
jokes.setup: Jane calls Danny 'cookie'
jokes.punchline: 'Is that what she calls you when she puts her penis in your vagina?' — a surreally anatomically inverted sex joke about Jane's dominance over Danny
jokes.explanation: The gender-inverted anatomy joke ('her penis in your vagina') is a sharp characterization of the Jane-Danny power dynamic. The surreal anatomy flip accurately captures Jane as the dominant force in any relationship. Sets up the excellent '14 years old' payoff.

jokes.text: Where have you been? / Why? / Did I miss the French toast dippy things? / You're two hours late. / No, that's impossible. I set my watch back for Daylight Savings Time.
jokes.setup: Jonah arrives very late to a second engagement
jokes.punchline: He set his watch THE WRONG DIRECTION for DST — 'back' instead of forward — making him two hours late instead of on time
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback — Jonah overcorrects from his earlier DST mistake and applies it wrong again, making himself two hours late instead of on time. The escalation of his incompetence is perfectly structured. 'Did I miss the French toast dippy things?' as his first concern is also perfectly Jonah.

jokes.text: Saving, not savings. / You set your watch the wrong way. / It's neither a plural, nor a possessive. / No, it's spring. I 'springed' backwards. / No, you spring forward! / Have ever watched girls gymnastics? That makes no sense.
jokes.setup: Jonah defends his DST error
jokes.punchline: His reasoning — 'I springed backwards, have you ever watched girls gymnastics? That makes no sense' — is a nonsensical sports analogy for a time convention
jokes.explanation: Jonah's 'girls gymnastics' defense is an inspired piece of Jonah-logic. He correctly identifies that gymnasts spring backwards (in a back handspring) and uses this to challenge the 'spring forward' convention. It's wrong-headed but internally consistent — which is exactly Jonah's voice. Very quotable. 'I springed backwards' is also a great grammatical failure.

jokes.text: You could just get the correct time from your phone. / Oh, could I? Well, guess what, my phone fell in the toilet, so who's the fucking smart guy now, Kent?
jokes.setup: Kent suggests the obvious solution to Jonah's watch problem
jokes.punchline: Jonah triumphantly announces his phone fell in the toilet as if this is Kent's problem
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jonah logic — he turns his own phone-in-toilet disaster into a rhetorical victory ('who's the fucking smart guy now?'). The confidence of the delivery is what makes this exceptional. The implication that this represents some kind of win over Kent is pure Jonah delusion. Very quotable.

jokes.text: Hey, hey, hey, listen to me, you plus-sized homunculus. / Plus-sized what? / Homunculus. A human-shaped creature of medieval legend that Paracelsus claimed was created from putrefied sperm. / How do you both know that?
jokes.setup: Ben calls Jonah a 'plus-sized homunculus'
jokes.punchline: Kent provides the precise definition — 'created from putrefied sperm' — which is horrifyingly apt for Jonah; Jonah's confused 'how do you both know that?' as if the word is obscure
jokes.explanation: One of the best jokes in the episode. The 'homunculus' insult is already inventive and character-specific (only Ben and Kent would deploy medieval alchemy terminology as a put-down). Kent's definition — 'created from putrefied sperm' — is both precise and hilariously appropriate as a Jonah description. Jonah's reaction ('How do you both know that?') is the perfect cherry on top — he's offended by the erudition, not the insult. The structure is three-stage perfection. This is genuinely 8+ territory.

jokes.text: If you don't want that reporter to write a story titled 'Granite State Represented by Twinless Tower,' then you better get over there, dip his cock in some maple syrup, and start sucking.
jokes.setup: Ben needs Jonah to do political outreach to a reporter
jokes.punchline: The instruction is delivered in a graphic sexual metaphor with a New Hampshire-specific detail (maple syrup)
jokes.explanation: 'Dip his cock in some maple syrup' is excellent — the New Hampshire regional flavoring of a graphic political instruction is very Veep. 'Twinless Tower' is also a sharp joke about Jonah's outsized frame (he's extremely tall). Good character integration for Ben's blunt political pragmatism.

jokes.text: If you don't want that reporter to write a story titled 'Granite State Represented by Twinless Tower,' then you better get over there, dip his cock in some maple syrup, and start sucking.
jokes.setup: Ben is urging Jonah to go charm the reporter; Jonah is resisting
jokes.punchline: 'Granite State Represented by Twinless Tower' as the headline (Jonah as a single tower — tall, looming, missing its twin) followed by the maple syrup instruction
jokes.explanation: 'Twinless Tower' is a brilliant Jonah insult that combines a 9/11 reference, the Twin Towers, Jonah's height, his loneliness, and his state (New Hampshire). The New Hampshire maple syrup detail in the crude metaphor is perfect regional specificity. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Take this down. / I'm not gonna suck your dick. The only one who should be sucking your dick is Daylight Savings Time. And I don't give a fuck if that means that some farmer in Kansas has to milk his cow using a fucking flashlight. I've had my own bedtime since I was 17. I didn't need a babysitter then and I don't need one now, and it's high time that the government stop trying to babysit all of us.
jokes.setup: Jonah decides to turn his DST personal grievance into a political statement for the reporter
jokes.punchline: His anti-DST rant is delivered with passionate incoherence — the 'farmer with a flashlight' is an inspired specific detail, and the whole speech accidentally becomes coherent political populism
jokes.explanation: The episode's big payoff for the DST running gag. Jonah's petty personal grievance about being embarrassed by DST transforms into a quasi-coherent anti-government populist speech. 'The only one who should be sucking your dick is Daylight Savings Time' is an inspired sentence. The farmer with a flashlight is specific and funny. The accidental coherence — it IS a reasonable anti-DST argument — is the joke: Jonah stumbles into valid policy through personal vanity. Sets up the legislation closing. One of the episode's best sustained pieces.

jokes.text: I'm not gonna suck your dick. The only one who should be sucking your dick is Daylight Savings Time.
jokes.setup: Jonah approaches the reporter and opens with a non-sequitur about DST
jokes.punchline: Jonah channels his DST frustration into a political speech that begins with 'I'm not gonna suck your dick' — directed at DST itself
jokes.explanation: The callback structure here is masterful: Ben told Jonah to suck the reporter's metaphorical dick; Jonah takes this literally, refuses, and redirects the sucking to Daylight Saving Time as a political entity. 'The only one who should be sucking your dick is Daylight Savings Time' is nonsense that somehow makes emotional sense given everything that's happened. The episode's DST thread reaches its perfect absurdist payoff. Justifies 8.5+.

jokes.text: That was all off the record.
jokes.setup: Jonah finishes his passionate anti-DST speech to the reporter
jokes.punchline: He immediately tries to take the whole thing off the record
jokes.explanation: Perfect coda to the speech — five words that complete the bit. Jonah's political instincts, however stunted, kick in too late. Economy is exceptional.

jokes.text: That was all off the record.
jokes.setup: Jonah has just delivered a full political speech to a reporter with a camera
jokes.punchline: Jonah thinks he can retroactively place an entire recorded speech 'off the record'
jokes.explanation: The 'that was all off the record' button is a perfect Jonah moment — it's barely a joke but it's exactly what he would say. His complete lack of media understanding after an impassioned on-camera speech is the character in microcosm.

jokes.text: Oh, my God, really? / Yeah. / We should be getting a phone call any minute. / Ooh, my heart is racing! / I bet this time, it's just menopause.
jokes.setup: Selina learns Montez is naming the nominee today
jokes.punchline: Gary — recovering from a heart attack — cracks that Selina's racing heart is 'just menopause' this time, inverting the running gag
jokes.explanation: Exceptional callback. Gary — barely recovered from his own heart attack, in Selina's house — turns the menopause gag back on Selina at the moment of maximum emotional tension. The inversion (now Gary making the menopause joke to Selina) perfectly completes the arc. Strong structure, high rewatch value for catching the callback resonance.

jokes.text: Get Shylock Tanz on the phone right now! / I think his name is Sherman Tanz.
jokes.setup: Selina learns she didn't get the Supreme Court nomination; pivots instantly back to Tanz for library money
jokes.punchline: 'Shylock Tanz' — Selina's anti-Semitic nickname for the Jewish billionaire; Richard's gentle 'I think his name is Sherman Tanz'
jokes.explanation: 'Shylock Tanz' is deeply in character for Selina — a casual literary anti-Semitic reference deployed the moment she no longer needs Tanz's political cover. Richard's gentle correction is the perfect foil. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Get Shylock Tanz on the phone right now! / What? / I don't know who Montez picked, but it sure as tits wasn't me.
jokes.setup: Selina learns she didn't get the Supreme Court nomination
jokes.punchline: She calls Tanz 'Shylock Tanz' (following 'Typhoid Moishe' — she has multiple offensive Jewish nicknames for him); 'sure as tits' as an oath
jokes.explanation: 'Shylock Tanz' as a second offensive nickname (after 'Typhoid Moishe') is a callback that builds on the pattern. 'Sure as tits' is a Selina-specific oath. The combination of the antisemitic nickname and the crude certainty expression is very dark Selina. Functional callback.

jokes.text: Fuck Montez and that fake bur... burrow... whatever you call those donkeys they ride in on. / Bureaus. / Burro?
jokes.setup: Selina expresses anger at Montez
jokes.punchline: She can't remember 'burro' — the Mexican donkey — while trying to make a dig about Montez being Mexican; the aide's 'Bureaus' correction is itself a malapropism (bureaus vs burros)
jokes.explanation: Selina's fumble for an ethnic slur she can't even remember is darkly funny — her racism is too scattered to even be competent. The aide's 'Bureaus' correction (wrong word) is a bonus layer. The structure is good.

jokes.text: It would've been a shame to hide that body under a robe. / You know? / Okay, yes.
jokes.setup: Selina is upset about not getting the Supreme Court nomination
jokes.punchline: An aide offers the consolation that it would have been a shame to cover Selina's figure in judicial robes — an absurd reason to miss the Supreme Court
jokes.explanation: The consolation prize being Selina's figure is a shallow but functional joke. The other aide's hesitant 'Okay, yes' (implying the consolation is thin) adds a layer. Competent.

jokes.text: It would've been a shame to hide that body under a robe.
jokes.setup: Selina has failed to get the Supreme Court seat
jokes.punchline: Someone consoles Selina by noting she has a good body that would be hidden under judicial robes — a deeply inappropriate compliment that lands as both a consolation and an insult
jokes.explanation: The consolation-as-objectification is a solid dark joke — consoling Selina about losing SCOTUS with 'your body would've been hidden' implies the robes are the real tragedy. It's the kind of thing that would come from someone who genuinely means it as kindness.

jokes.text: I want you to remind me what it is exactly that you're getting. / Jonah Ryan. He's young, he's raw, he's a fresh, if slightly deformed, face that I can ride like a hobby horse.
jokes.setup: Tanz says he gets something before Selina finishes pitching
jokes.punchline: Tanz has figured out he's getting Jonah; his description — 'slightly deformed face that I can ride like a hobby horse' — is a perfect Tanz encapsulation of political puppetry
jokes.explanation: Tanz's 'hobby horse' description of Jonah is excellent political satire — the wealthy donor's view of a politician as a rideable toy is perfectly expressed. 'Slightly deformed' is a funny qualifier that's accurate to Jonah's character. Strong character work for Tanz.

jokes.text: Did you see his nonsense about the Daylight Savings? / I love this guy.
jokes.setup: Tanz is enthusiastic about Jonah
jokes.punchline: His specific reason for loving Jonah is the DST nonsense — the very incompetence that embarrassed Jonah is what makes him valuable to Tanz
jokes.explanation: Tanz loving Jonah specifically for the DST nonsense is a perfect satirical callback — the chaos that embarrassed Jonah is precisely what made him an attractive populist tool. It validates the entire DST running gag as having political consequences. Excellent structural payoff for the episode's thematic thread.

jokes.text: Yeah, that was the fucking point, Sherman. Bye!
jokes.setup: Tanz explains he's now backing Jonah as a favor to Selina
jokes.punchline: Selina's furious 'that was the fucking point' acknowledges that yes, she sent Jonah TO be used by Tanz, but she never expected Tanz to actually LIKE him — her plan worked too well
jokes.explanation: Selina's explosion reveals the full comedy of her plan: she weaponized Jonah's awfulness, but Tanz found it charming. The Byronically quick 'Bye!' after a revelation this significant is pure Selina.

jokes.text: I am all in on Jonah Ryan, and I owe you one. / Yeah, that was the fucking point, Sherman. / Bye!
jokes.setup: Tanz thanks Selina and cuts the library contribution
jokes.punchline: Selina's barely contained rage at Tanz's 'I owe you one' — she knows 'owing one' is the entire currency of this relationship, and she wanted cash
jokes.explanation: 'Yeah, that was the fucking point, Sherman' and 'Bye!' — Selina's clipped rage expressed in two sentences — is excellent. The political transaction that left her with nothing but a favor is devastating comic punctuation.

jokes.text: Aw, well, at least you made the short list. / How are you feeling, by the way? / Good. / Then shut the fuck up.
jokes.setup: Gary offers Selina consolation about not getting the nomination
jokes.punchline: 'Then shut the fuck up' — Selina dismisses Gary's 'how are you feeling' inquiry the instant he gives a positive answer
jokes.explanation: Perfectly structured two-beat joke. Gary asking how Selina is feeling is a gesture of care; Selina's instant shutdown ('Good. Then shut the fuck up.') is both funny and characterologically precise — she only wants to perform vulnerability, not actually discuss it. The beat between 'Good' and 'Then shut the fuck up' is immaculate. Very quotable.

jokes.text: On the bright side, it's gonna be an amazing new exhibit at the Hughes Library.
jokes.setup: Gary is still trying to console Selina about the Hughes nomination
jokes.punchline: Gary accidentally frames the competitor's library (Hughes Library) as the one that will benefit from the story — he's consoling Selina by pointing to her rival's gain
jokes.explanation: Gary's accidental consolation pointing to a rival's benefit is a good ironic joke. His obliviousness to what he's actually saying is very Gary. The library running thread gets a dark coda.

jokes.text: Wait, do I have to read all this?
jokes.setup: Jonah is handed a full piece of legislation prepared by Tanz's team
jokes.punchline: His first response to receiving major legislation is whether he has to read it
jokes.explanation: Maximum economy — five words that summarize Jonah's relationship with governance. A legislator asking if he has to read legislation is a perfect character beat. The joke is familiar but Jonah's delivery makes it work.

jokes.text: Mr. Speaker, Daylight Saving... not plural... Time has never saved us from anything, certainly not the higher energy costs it promised to lower.
jokes.setup: Jonah delivers his Tanz-written anti-DST speech on the floor of Congress
jokes.punchline: He begins by pedantically correcting himself mid-title ('not plural') — showing he's absorbed the Kent correction from earlier in the episode
jokes.explanation: The '...not plural...' parenthetical is a beautiful callback — Jonah has absorbed exactly one thing from his staff's corrections (the plural vs. non-plural distinction) and deploys it in a Congressional speech. The contrast between this nitpick and the actual substance of the speech is perfectly Jonah. Great payoff for the episode-long DST running gag.

jokes.text: This bill will be a call for less government from our clocks to our privately built for-profit prisons to the very watches on our wrists.
jokes.setup: Jonah is delivering the Tanz-written anti-government speech
jokes.punchline: The speech smoothly connects DST libertarianism to for-profit prisons — Tanz has successfully laundered his prison agenda inside an anti-government populist speech about time zones
jokes.explanation: The episode's structural comedic payoff — Tanz's prison lobbying and Jonah's DST mishap have been stitched together into actual legislation. The specific insertion of 'privately built for-profit prisons' into a DST speech is the satirical thesis of the episode delivered as a punchline. This is the show's political satire at its most incisive and structurally satisfying. The 'earned' score is very high because the entire episode built to this.

tmdb_overview: Selina and her team attend the funeral of a Supreme Court Justice. Amy works on securing a location for Selina's library. Dan tries to maintain his upswing with his co-host. Jonah finds an issue.
insight: Political ambition collapses under procedural tedium; 87 jokes sustain the entropy.
